---
title: "The Ballon d'Or Rewards Goals — and Stories."
subtitle: "You mostly can't separate the two — except at the stage that decides who gets considered."
author: "Valter Nunez"
date: "2026-06-24"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
    code-tools: false
    html-math-method: plain   # no real math here; drops Quarto's own MathJax
    include-in-header:
      # Plotly bundles its own MathJax v2, which prints a visible "MathZoom.js failed to load"
      # banner on init. There's no LaTeX in any figure, so suppress the message + extension load.
      text: |
        <script>window.MathJax={messageStyle:"none",showProcessingMessages:false,showMathMenu:false};</script>
        <style>#MathJax_Message{display:none !important;}</style>
        <link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="data:image/svg+xml;base64,">
jupyter: bdor
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{python}
# | label: setup
# | output: false
# Silence library import chatter (soccerdata / arviz / pytensor "g++ not available") so it doesn't
# leak into the rendered report — these are logged to stderr, which echo/warning options don't catch.
import logging
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
logging.disable(logging.WARNING)

from bdor import report

stats = report.headline_stats()
A, B = stats["gate_a"], stats["gate_b"]
E = report.effect_sizes(stats)        # odds ratios + Gate-A predicted-probability effect
RX = report.robustness_extras()       # bootstrap (generated-regressor) + Heckman rows
```

Every December, football argues about one trophy. Did the right player win the Ballon d'Or? The
complaint is always some version of the same thing: *that was a story, not a season.* The winner had
a narrative — a World Cup, a transfer saga, a fairy-tale rise — and the voters fell for it.

It is a satisfying accusation, and a hard one to test, because **hype and merit are closely tied**:
players get talked about *because* they produce. So the question here is not a "hype vs. merit" exposé
but a narrower one:

> **Does media attention have *independent* explanatory power for the Ballon d'Or — after you control
> for what a player actually did on the pitch and how their team performed?**

If attention still predicts the award once production and team success are held constant, that is the
bias. If it does not, then "they're inseparable" is itself the answer. Seven years of data
(2018–2025, the years with clean expected-goals coverage) point to a specific answer.

## How you measure a "story"

Two ingredients, both built to be conservative.

**Merit.** For every attacking player–season I standardize the expected and actual output — non-penalty
xG, expected assists, buildup involvement, goals, assists — *within position and season*, so "good"
means "good for your role this year." Two PCA axes summarize it. The generosity is deliberate: the more
merit absorbs, the more credible whatever remains.

**Attention beyond merit — "H⊥".** I take each player's all-language Wikipedia pageviews over the
qualifying window (cut *before* the shortlist is announced, so the award can't leak backwards into the
predictor), and I strip out the parts of attention that *aren't* narrative: baseline fame (Messi pulls
10× the traffic of a full-back every day), performance-driven attention, and the Champions-League /
trophy bump. **What remains — the residual — is H⊥: attention a player got that their merit and team
don't explain.** It is, literally, the part of attention a regression cannot explain. (On the companion
[scrollytelling site](../site/index.html) this same quantity is called the **Hype Score** — same number,
friendlier name.)

A high H⊥ does not mean a player is bad; it means they were talked about *more than their on-pitch case
warrants*. A negative H⊥ is the reverse: a strong season that drew little attention.

One important qualifier, stated up front: H⊥ is attention beyond the merit I can *measure*. For roles the
public data captures well — attackers — that residual really is buzz. For roles it captures poorly — a
deep-lying playmaker, a positional center-back whose value is in the actions he *prevents* — some of the
residual may be genuine on-pitch value the box score misses, read as narrative because nothing in the
data accounts for it. I flag those cases where they arise rather than treat the merit index as complete.

```{python}
# | label: fig-leaderboard
# | fig-cap: "H⊥ among Ballon d'Or finishers. Red = more attention than merit explains; blue = elite production that flew under the radar."
# | fig-alt: "Diverging horizontal bar chart ranking finishers by H⊥: wonderkids and breakout stories at the positive end, under-the-radar elite producers (Lewandowski, De Bruyne, Jorginho) at the negative end."
report.fig_leaderboard().show(config={"displayModeBar": False, "responsive": True})
```

The leaderboard is face-valid. The over-attended end is **wonderkids and breakout stories** — a
16-year-old Euro-winning Lamine Yamal, Kvaratskhelia's debut-season explosion, teenage Pedri, a €50m
Désiré Doué. The under-the-radar end is **elite producers who never drew the volume their numbers
warranted** — Lewandowski, a peak-years De Bruyne, and Jorginho, who won the Champions League *and* the
Euros in 2021 with strikingly little attention.

## The finding: the bias is in who gets *considered*

The award is really two decisions. First, a 30-man shortlist (**Gate A — do you get noticed?**). Then,
a vote that ranks them (**Gate B — given you're in, do you finish higher?**). So I ask the H⊥ question
*twice*, once at each gate, controlling for merit and team success both times.

```{python}
# | label: fig-two-gate
# | fig-cap: "The H⊥ coefficient at each gate (94% credible interval). Both controlled for merit + team success."
# | fig-alt: "Forest plot: H⊥'s coefficient is large and clearly positive at the nomination gate and small (interval near zero) at the placement gate."
report.fig_two_gate().show(config={"displayModeBar": False, "responsive": True})
```

One chart carries the result.

- **At the nomination gate, narrative matters a lot.** H⊥'s effect is large and effectively certain
  (`{python} f"{A['mean']:+.2f}"`, 94% interval `{python} f"[{A['lo']:.2f}, {A['hi']:.2f}]"`,
  probability positive `{python} (">99%" if A['p_pos'] >= 0.995 else f"{A['p_pos']:.0%}")`). Being talked about *beyond what you did* makes
  you meaningfully more likely to make the 30 — even after merit and team success are in the model.
- **At the placement gate, it matters much less** (`{python} f"{B['mean']:+.2f}"`,
  `{python} f"[{B['lo']:.2f}, {B['hi']:.2f}]"`). Once you're in the room, the vote is driven far more
  by production and trophies; narrative adds only a modest nudge.

**In plain terms** (log-odds are not intuitive): at equal merit and team success, a
one-standard-deviation rise in Hype Score **roughly `{python} f"{E['gate_a_or']['or']:.1f}"`× the odds
of being shortlisted** — about **+`{python} f"{E['gate_a_ame']['mean']:.0f}"` percentage points**,
lifting the average candidate from ~`{python} f"{E['gate_a_ame']['base_pct']:.0f}"`% to
~`{python} f"{E['gate_a_ame']['base_pct'] + E['gate_a_ame']['mean']:.0f}"`%. For placement the same jump
moves the odds only ~`{python} f"{E['gate_b_or']['or']:.2f}"`× (about
`{python} f"+{(E['gate_b_or']['or'] - 1) * 100:.0f}"`%). On the shared per-SD log-odds scale that's the
~`{python} f"{E['ratio']:.0f}"`× gap the chart shows — directional, since the two gates are different
likelihoods (a yes/no shortlist vs a vote share).

This was the working hypothesis going in. The Ballon d'Or's attention bias lies mainly in who gets
*considered* rather than who *wins*: the shortlist, the stage the public never sees, is where narrative
does most of its work. Merit still dominates nomination, and because H⊥ is orthogonal to merit by
construction, the effect is genuine narrative lift rather than merit in disguise.

## The cases everyone argues about

```{python}
# | label: tbl-cases
# | tbl-cap: "Four contested years, by the numbers."
cases = report.case_studies()
cases = cases.rename(columns={
    "player": "Player", "award_year": "Year", "label": "The story",
    "rank": "Finish", "h_perp_pv": "H⊥", "merit_pc1": "Merit (PC1)"})
cases[["Player", "Year", "Finish", "Merit (PC1)", "H⊥", "The story"]].round(2)
```

**Modrić, 2018.** A fine season and a World Cup final, paired with the single highest narrative residual
of any *winner* in the sample. The data agrees with the complaint, with one qualification: Modrić is a
deep-lying playmaker, the role the merit index reads *least* well, so part of that residual may be value
the box score cannot see rather than narrative.

**Lewandowski, 2019.** The opposite case, and a cleaner one: one of the highest *merit* scores in seven
years — a relentless goalscorer — paired with a *negative* H⊥. Elite production, little attention, and
eighth place — the underrated profile made measurable.

**Messi, 2023 vs. Lewandowski, 2019.** The two have nearly identical elite merit and opposite H⊥ — the
same production with very different narratives. That contrast is the analysis in two points:

```{python}
# | label: fig-scatter
# | fig-cap: "Merit vs. attention among finishers (colour = H⊥). Two players, near-identical production, opposite buzz."
# | fig-alt: "Scatter of finishers, merit on x and log attention on y, coloured by H⊥; Messi 2023 and Lewandowski 2019 are annotated at near-identical merit with opposite H⊥."
report.fig_merit_vs_attention().show(config={"displayModeBar": False, "responsive": True})
```

## Is it real, or an artifact?

With a sample this small, one specification settles little. So I re-estimated the H⊥ effect under
deliberately varied choices — dropping the Messi/Ronaldo duopoly, dropping breakout newcomers, leaving
out each year in turn.

```{python}
# | label: fig-robustness
# | fig-cap: "The H⊥ coefficient across specifications. Gate A barely moves; Gate B is smaller and leans partly on the duopoly."
# | fig-alt: "Caterpillar plot faceted by gate: Gate A's H⊥ estimate clusters tightly away from zero across every specification; Gate B's is smaller with wider, lower intervals."
report.fig_robustness().show(config={"displayModeBar": False, "responsive": True})
```

The nomination effect is **very stable** — it sits in the same place across every cut, and no single
year or pair of superstars drives it. The placement effect is stable but smaller, and leans partly on
Messi and Ronaldo: drop them and its interval grazes zero. The firm result is therefore the *nomination*
effect; the placement effect is real but should be stated cautiously.

Three further checks point the same way. **Capping the calendar window at the ceremony date** (so not even a
single post-ceremony match can sneak in) leaves both gates essentially unchanged (Gate A
`{python} f"{RX['strict_a']['est']:+.2f}"`, Gate B `{python} f"{RX['strict_b']['est']:+.2f}"`). Because
H⊥ is itself an *estimated* residual, I **bootstrapped the whole pipeline** — re-estimating H⊥ on
resampled data and refitting both gates each time, so the intervals carry that first-stage uncertainty
too — and Gate A stays far from zero (95% CI
`{python} f"[{RX['bootstrap_a']['lo']:.2f}, {RX['bootstrap_a']['hi']:.2f}]"`) while Gate B stays positive
(`{python} f"[{RX['bootstrap_b']['lo']:.2f}, {RX['bootstrap_b']['hi']:.2f}]"`). Finally, a **Heckman
selection control** — modelling who gets nominated, then correcting the placement fit for it — keeps the
placement effect the same sign (`{python} f"{RX['heckman_b']['est']:+.2f}"`), albeit with a wide interval
that crosses zero. With no clean exclusion restriction and a sample this size, that last check is a
sign test rather than a clean correction; the two-gate design is the real response to selection, since
the bias falls at the gate modelled directly.

A final check shows why the windowing matters. The attention window is cut *before* the ceremony. If it
is allowed to run *past* the ceremony — catching the winner-announcement pageview spike — the placement
effect inflates, exactly as a leak should, while nomination (decided weeks earlier) does not move.
Attention is strongly event-driven, which is why the cut has to be disciplined:

```{python}
# | label: fig-spike
# | fig-cap: "All-language daily pageviews for the 2024 winner (Rodri). The window ends at the shortlist cut — the big winner-announcement spike lands after it, and is excluded."
# | fig-alt: "Daily pageview line for Rodri (2024) with window markers: a large spike sits at the ceremony, to the right of (after) the shortlist-cut line that ends the measurement window."
report.fig_spike().show(config={"displayModeBar": False, "responsive": True})
```

## What this is, and isn't

A few limits:

- **Small N.** Seven award years; this is an *interpretation* rather than a prediction engine, and the
  intervals are deliberately wide.
- **Merit by role, unevenly.** Attackers get the fullest merit (Understat production); midfielders a
  ball-winning score, keepers a shot-stopping one, center-backs a tackle/aerial/passing one. So the
  nomination test (Gate A) is attackers-only, while placement (Gate B) now includes defenders and
  keepers. The remaining gap: purely-positional center-backs (a Rúben Dias) still read low — great
  positioning leaves *fewer* actions to count, and no public stat captures it — so part of their H⊥ may
  be unmeasured value rather than narrative.
- **One attention proxy.** Wikipedia pageviews, all languages. A news-volume proxy (GDELT) is a
  natural second opinion and is future work. Pageviews can also track a player's national-market size
  or diaspora; baseline fame absorbs the steady part, but some residual market effect may survive.
- **Selection at Gate B.** The placement model only sees players who were nominated, for which a
  Heckman correction is the principled fix — applied here as a check rather than a definitive
  correction.

## So — goals or stories?

Both — and you can mostly tell them apart, though not at the point where the arguments usually happen.
By the time the votes are counted, production and trophies are doing most of the work, and the winner is
rarely a pure confection. The bias sits one step earlier, in the quiet decision about **who even gets on
the list**. Narrative's main effect is on who reaches the shortlist, not on who wins.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Methods & reproducibility
Spine: 2018, 2019, 2021–2025 (2020 cancelled; 2017 pre-xG). Performance from Understat; outcomes from
the Wikipedia results tables; attention from the Wikimedia pageviews API summed across all language
editions. Merit = within-position-season standardized production (PCA), built from **match-level data
summed over each award year's official performance window** so calendar-regime years (2018/2019/2021)
don't credit matches played after the ceremony; the season-aggregate defensive/keeper signals, which
have no match logs, fall back to the most recent *completed* season (decided before the ceremony, but
a coarser window than the match-level attacking merit — the public-data ceiling). H⊥ = residual of
log-window-attention on log-baseline + merit + club team-success, fit pool-wide. International
tournament success is **not** a de-fame regressor — a player→nation mapping exists only for finishers,
so including it would residualize inconsistently across the full candidate pool — and so enters only
the placement model (Gate B), where every row is a finisher. Outcome models are
Bayesian (a Beta regression for vote share, a logistic for nomination, year partial-pooling, fit with
nutpie); the robustness panel uses fast frequentist anchors that track the posteriors. Everything is
cache-first and reproducible via `python run.py <stage>`.
:::
